1: 'Woe to those who devise wickedness and work evil on their beds! When the morning dawns, they carry it out, because it is in the power of their hand.' The opening oracle of ch. 2 identifies the social enemy: the powerful landowner who plans land seizures at night and executes them by day. The nighttime planning echoes Ps 4:4 ('meditate in your hearts upon your beds') inverted: they meditate on injustice rather than on YHWH. The power to execute the plan immediately (ki yesh-le'el yadam, 'for it is in the power of their hand') is itself identified as dangerous — wealth that translates instantly into legal and physical coercion.
2: 'They covet fields and seize them, and houses, and take them away; they oppress a man and his house, a man and his inheritance (nachalah).' The verb chamad (covet) directly cites the tenth commandment (Exod 20:17; Deut 5:21). This is the Decalogue applied to land theft. The word nachalah (inheritance/ancestral allotment) is theologically loaded: in Israel's land theology, the tribal allotments were YHWH's gift held in trust for family lines — they could not be permanently sold away (Lev 25; cf. Naboth's vineyard, 1 Kgs 21). Seizing a man's nachalah is both economic crime and theological violation.
3: 'Therefore thus says YHWH: behold, against this family I am devising disaster.' YHWH counters the nighttime evil-devising with his own counter-devise (choschev) — using the exact same verb. The disaster from which they cannot escape or remove their necks will humble their proud gait. The 'family' (mishpachah) targeted may refer to the ruling class as a whole or specifically to the northern dynasty.
4: 'In that day they shall take up a taunt against you and moan bitterly, and say, “We are utterly ruined; he changes the portion of my people; how he removes it from me! To an apostate he allots our fields.”' The lamentation they will utter over themselves anticipates the reversal: the land-grabbers will be lamented over by themselves as their own lands are redistributed. 'To an apostate he allots our fields' — the Assyrian conqueror (or divine judgment acting through him) will redistribute their ill-gotten estates.
5: 'Therefore you will have none to cast the line by lot in the assembly of YHWH.' The casting of the line (chevel begoral) refers to the measurement cord used in land allotment (cf. Josh 17:5; Ps 16:6). The punishment mirrors the crime: those who stole others' measured allotments will have no one to measure out land for them in the coming restoration. They are excluded from the eschatological re-distribution of the land.
6: 'Do not preach' — thus they preach — 'one should not preach of such things; disgrace will not overtake us.' The false prophets and their patrons silence Micah: 'Don't prophesy!' (al-tatifun). The word nataf (drip/prophesy) is used in Amos 7:16 similarly. The self-exemption from disgrace ('disgrace will not overtake us') is the standard psychology of those who believe their social power protects them from divine accountability. Micah has been suppressed — he reports their suppression.
7: 'Should this be said, O house of Jacob? Has the Spirit of YHWH grown impatient? Are these his deeds? Do not my words do good to him who walks uprightly?' Micah turns the false prophets' theology against them. If YHWH's words are only comfort and the land-grabbers are comfortable, then YHWH's Spirit has run out of patience for justice. But YHWH's words do good to those who walk uprightly — implying that those who hear only good news from their prophets are not walking uprightly.
8: 'But lately my people have risen up as an enemy; you strip the rich robe from those who pass by trustingly, like men returning from war.' The social violence is described vividly: ordinary people (perhaps creditors or land-seizing nobles) strip the outer cloak (aderet) from passersby — the very act prohibited in Exod 22:26 (a creditor may not keep a poor man's cloak overnight). 'Like men returning from war' — those who expect to be safe, returning home, are instead attacked by their own countrymen.
9: 'The women of my people you drive out from their pleasant houses; from their young children you take away my splendor forever.' Women (widows or wives) are evicted from their homes. The children lose their glory (hadar) — their inheritance, their social dignity, their future. 'Forever' (le'olam): Micah emphasizes the permanence of the damage done to children by landlessness. This is generational poverty built into the social structure.
10: 'Arise and go, for this is no place to rest, because of uncleanness that destroys with grievous destruction.' The land itself vomits out the unclean. The language echoes Lev 18:25-28: the land vomits out its inhabitants for their wickedness. This is the land's own sacred ecology — a concept unique to Israel's theology where the land is not morally neutral but responds to the behavior of its inhabitants.
11: 'If a man should go about in a spirit of falsehood and lie, saying, “I will preach to you of wine and strong drink,” he would be the preacher for this people!' The bitter irony: the land-grabbing elite want a prophet who will prophesy them wine and beer rather than judgment. Micah identifies the market for false prophecy: not spiritual error but economic convenience. A prophet who tells powerful people what they want to hear has a reliable patron base — and no shortage of clients.
12: Micah inserts a restoration oracle into the midst of judgment: 'I will surely assemble all of you, O Jacob; I will gather the remnant of Israel; I will set them together like sheep in a fold, like a flock in its pasture, a noisy multitude of men.' The sudden shift to salvation — typical of the prophetic alternation of judgment and hope — names the coming gathering of a remnant (she'erit Yisrael). The metaphor of the noisy flock packed into a fold contrasts with the scattered exile of judgment.
13: 'He who opens the breach goes up before them; they break through and pass the gate, going out by it. Their king passes on before them, YHWH at their head.' The 'breach-breaker' (poretz) who leads the flock is a royal/messianic figure. The image is of a shepherd breaking down a pen wall to lead sheep out — the same metaphor that appears in John 10:3 (the shepherd calls his own and leads them out). YHWH himself goes at the head of this procession, the exodus-restoration merger of leadership that characterizes Micah's eschatology.
